title: practical english usage - Part 347
content:
She didn't run quite fast enough for a record.

I'm not quite ready - won't be a minute.

adjectives, adverbs, verbs and nouns, including nouns with the.

Not quite means 'not completely' or 'not exactly'. It can be used before 5 not    quite 566 vocabulary' •         rather, preference ►    4so and rather,      quite,     pretty          fairly I suppose I'd better clean the windows, but I'd rather watch TV.

(= should, ►    77). Compare: Note that would rather (= would prefer) is not the same as had better 5                                and would       rather           had     better He's a psychologist - or rather, a psychoanalyst.

People often use or rather to correct themselves.

For other structures where a past tense has a present or future meaning, ►    46.

This structure is still found in grammars, but it is not normally used.

In older English, had rather was used in the same way as would rather.

I wish you hadn't done that.

However, this kind of idea is usually expressed with I wish (►  632).

I'd rather you hadn't done that.

is possible.

home now), but unusual. To  talk about past actions, a past perfect tense A present tense or present subjunctive is possible (e.g. I'd rather he goes / he go 'Shall I open a window? 'I'd rather you didn't.' My wife would rather we didn't see each other any more.

Tomorrow's difficult. I'd rather you came next weekend.

I'd rather you went home now.

would rather + subject + past tense something. We   use a special structure with a past tense.

We   can use would rather to say that a person would prefer somebody to do 3                                   past tense with  present or future  meaning would       rather: have a glass of beer.’ (= I’ d prefer ...) 'I'd rather like a cup of coffee.’ (= I’ d quite like ...) 'Oh, would you? I'd rather expression, rather means ‘ quite’ , and does not suggest preference. Compare: Note that would rather like (BrE) does not mean ‘ would prefer’ ; in this Would sooner is sometimes used in the same way as would rather.

I'd rather not go out tonight, (     I wouldn't rather. ..) The  negative is would rather not.

'How about a drink? 'I'd rather have something to eat!

Would you rather stay here or go home?

without to. We   often use the contraction 'd rather.

(= She is like her mother.) We   can also use remind.. . of to talk about similarities.

Remind me of your phone number.

(     .. . reminds me our old house-. ..) The smell of hay always reminds me of our old house in the country.

the past, or things that have been forgotten.

We   use remind.. . of to say that something/somebody makes us remember 3   remind       . .. of  . ..

I reminded him  that we hadn't got any bread left.

Please remind me to go to the bank, (     Please remind me of going.. .) (for facts).

After remind, we  can use an infinitive structure (for actions) or a that-dause 2 structures For remember + -ing form or infinitive, ►     105.1 (= 'Give my  best wishes to your parents’ ) and similar sentences.

But note the special use of remember in Remember me to your parents This sort of weather makes me remember my home.

Reminding somebody means 'making 1  meaning:      remind        and   remember 568  re  mind      and   rem    ember        _________ reason (as in Sorry I'm late - the reason is because I overslept).

Some people consider it incorrect to use a because-clause as a complement after The reason she doesn't like me is that I make her nervous.

In an informal style, why/that is often left out.

Do you know the reason that they’ re closing the factory?
